mainTopic: (continues) תני רבי חייא מנה לי בידך · ואידך דרבי חייא
overview: (1) The Gemara analyzes Rabbi Chiya's teaching on partial admission and witnesses, exploring the kal vachomer logic of Rav Papa regarding why witnesses obligate an oath just as a partial admission does. (2) The Gemara discusses the alternative opinion of Rabbi Chiya regarding the phrase 'hilech' (here it is) and reconciles it with Rav Sheshet's view that such a statement exempts from an oath, attributing the Mishnah's oath to a rabbinic enactment.

sugyaBoundaries.topic: (continues) תני רבי חייא מנה לי בידך
sugyaBoundaries.gist: The Gemara analyzes Rabbi Chiya's teaching on partial admission and witnesses, exploring the kal vachomer logic of Rav Papa regarding why witnesses obligate an oath just as a partial admission does.

The Gemara discusses the alternative opinion of Rabbi Chiya regarding the phrase 'hilech' (here it is) and reconciles it with Rav Sheshet's view that such a statement exempts from an oath, attributing the Mishnah's oath to a rabbinic enactment.

steps.title: Resolution via גְּלִיגַל שְׁבוּעָה
steps.stepSummary: Rav Pappa resolves the objection by deriving the law from the Gilgul Shevuah of a single witness, where one oath leads to another.
steps.whatsHappening: רַב פָּפָא answers the objection by shifting the derivation to the concept of גְּלִיגַל שְׁבוּעָה. A single witness obligates an oath, and this oath can be extended to other claims.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The resolution changes the focus from the direct testimony to the extension of the oath. The single witness triggers an oath, and this oath can be 'rolled' to cover other claims. This mechanism applies to two witnesses as well, bypassing the scope objection.

keyTerms.meaning: Extension/rolling of an oath to other claims.
Amora who provides this resolution.

steps.whatToRemember: The derivation is from the גְּלִיגַל שְׁבוּעָה mechanism.
steps.confusionAlert: גְּלִיגַל שְׁבוּעָה is a specific legal concept where one oath triggers another.
steps.whyThisMatters: It shows how legal mechanisms can bridge gaps in קַל וָחוֹמֶר derivations.

phrases.english: Rather, Rav Pappa said:
It comes from the extension of an oath of one witness.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא אָמַר רַב פָּפָּא: אָתֵי מִגִּלְגּוּל שְׁבוּעָה דְּעֵד אֶחָד

rashi.hebrew: מגלגול שבועה דעד אחד - אם נתחייב לו שבועה ע"י עד אחד כדאמרינן והיתה עליו טענה אחרת שלא היתה מוטלת עליו שבועה מגלגלין אותה עם שבועה זאת ונשבע על שניהן דגלגול שבועה דאורייתא היא דילפינן לה בקדושין (דף כז:) מואמרה האשה אמן אמן הרי שעל מה שלא העיד העד הוא משביעו:
rashi.english: From the extension of an oath due to the testimony of one witness - if he had been obligated to take an oath due to the testimony of one witness, as we have stated, and there was another claim against him for which no oath had been imposed, we may extend this oath onto him and make him swear to both of them. The extension of an oath is from Torah law, as is derived in Kiddushin [Kiddushin 27b:5] from "and the woman shall say: Amen, amen" [Numbers 5:22] -- one can be made to swear even regarding that about which the witness did not testify.
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that גלגול שבועה is a Torah-level mechanism derived from קידושין, allowing an oath triggered by a single witness to be extended to other claims not directly testified to.

steps.title: Challenge from nature of גְּלִיגַל
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the Gilgul derivation: Gilgul is an oath triggering another oath, whereas witnesses directly obligate payment.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises an objection to the גְּלִיגַל derivation. It points out that גְּלִיגַל שְׁבוּעָה is a case where one oath leads to another oath.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This objection attacks the גְּלִיגַל derivation by highlighting a difference in the nature of the obligation. גְּלִיגַל is an indirect obligation, while witnesses create a direct monetary obligation. This difference suggests the קַל וָחוֹמֶר is invalid.

keyTerms.meaning: An oath that triggers another oath.
They obligate monetary payment.

steps.whatToRemember: The objection focuses on the indirect vs. direct nature of the obligations.
steps.confusionAlert: The distinction between 'שְׁבוּעָה גוֹרֶרֶת שְׁבוּעָה' and 'מָמוֹן קָא מַחְיֵיבִי' is key.
steps.whyThisMatters: It tests the קַל וָחוֹמֶר by checking if the source and target cases share the same type of obligation.

phrases.english: What is notable about the extension of an oath of one witness?
A neighbor's oath drags along an oath.
Would you say the same with regard to witnesses?
That they are obligating with regard to money!

steps.triggerLanguage: מָה לְגִלְגּוּל שְׁבוּעָה דְּעֵד אֶחָד, שֶׁכֵּן שְׁבוּעָה גּוֹרֶרֶת שְׁבוּעָה

rashi.hebrew: שכן שבועה גוררת שבועה - עד לא חייבו שבועה אלא על מה שהעיד והשבועה גוררת שבועה תאמר בעדים הללו שהחמשים שהעידוהו הם מחייבין אותו לשלם ואין כאן שבועה לגרור על ידה שבועה על השאר:
rashi.english: That an oath leads to an oath - the witness only obligates him to take an oath regarding that about which he testified; it is the oath that leads to [the second] oath. Would you then say the same regarding these witnesses? They would obligate him to pay the fifty about which they testified, but there is no oath here that could be used to lead to another oath regarding the rest of the sum.
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the objection from גלגול is valid because גלגול involves an oath triggering another oath, whereas witnesses directly obligate monetary payment without an initial oath to trigger further liability.

steps.title: Resolution via combined proof
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the objection by combining the admission and single witness cases, using each to prove the other's missing element.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא answers the objection by combining the admission and עֵד אֶחָד cases. The admission proves that an oath can be obligated even when not subject to הַכְחָשָׁה.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The resolution uses a combined proof. The admission shows that an oath can be obligated even without הַכְחָשָׁה. The עֵד אֶחָד shows that an oath can be obligated even with הַכְחָשָׁה. By combining them, the קַל וָחוֹמֶר is strengthened, as both cases contribute unique elements that cover the objections.

keyTerms.meaning: The admission can prove.
The single witness can prove.

steps.whatToRemember: The resolution combines the admission and עֵד אֶחָד to cover all legal aspects.
steps.confusionAlert: This is a complex resolution that uses two sources to support each other.
steps.whyThisMatters: It shows how multiple sources can be combined to overcome objections to a קַל וָחוֹמֶר.

phrases.aramaic: פִּיו יוֹכִיחַ. מָה לְפִיו שֶׁכֵּן אֵינוֹ בְּהַכְחָשָׁה –
phrases.english: His mouth will prove. What is his mouth, a neighbor is not in denial –

phrases.aramaic: עֵד אֶחָד יוֹכִיחַ שֶׁיֶּשְׁנוֹ בְּהַכְחָשָׁה וּמְחַיְּיבוֹ שְׁבוּעָה.
phrases.english: One witness will prove that he is in denial and obligates him an oath.
What is one witness,

phrases.aramaic: שֶׁכֵּן עַל מַה שֶּׁמֵּעִיד הוּא נִשְׁבָּע,
phrases.english: A neighbor on what he testifies he swears,
Would you say with witnesses –

phrases.aramaic: שֶׁעַל מַה שֶּׁכָּפַר הוּא נִשְׁבָּע! פִּיו יוֹכִיחַ.
phrases.english: That on what he denied he swears! His mouth will prove.

rashi.hebrew: פיו יוכיח - מודה מקצת הטענה שעל מה שכופר ופיו לא הודה הוא נשבע:
rashi.english: One's mouth can prove - [that is,] one who admits to part of the claim -- he swears regarding that portion which he denied -- about which his mouth did not admit.
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that פיו יוכיח refers to מודה מקצת, where the defendant swears an oath regarding the portion he denied, which his own mouth did not admit to.

tosafot.hebrew: עד אחד יוכיח שישנו בהכחשה ובהזמה - שפיר גרס הכא ובהזמה כיון שע"י הזמה דבורו בטל ואין להקשות מה לפיו שכן אינו משלם בהזמה תאמר בעדים שמשלמין בהזמה ואז לא יכול לומר עד אחד יוכיח שמשלם כי מה שהעד אינו משלם כשהוא מוזם אינו חומרא אלא לפי שבעדותו אינו מחייבו ממון לכך הואיל ודיבורו בטל חשיב ישנו בהזמה ובסמוך על מה הצד פרכינן ליה משום דעל מה הצד פרכינן פירכא כל דהו ורבי חייא אפי' על מה הצד לא פריך ליה משום דמה שאינו משלם העד כשמוזם זהו גריעותו לפי שלא היה כח בעדותו לחייב ממון:
tosafot.english: [The oath obligated by] a single witness which is subject to contradiction and hazamah1See Artscroll 3B3 note 23 for a definition of hazamah. demonstrates [that an oath can be obligated by testimony which is subject to nullification by contradiction and hazamah]. For proper understanding of this Tosfos we must keep in mind: Hazamah has a double function. It causes a) nullification of the testimony and also b) obligates the zomemim witnesses to pay their intended victim what their testimony would have cost him. For the sake of clarity, we will mark each reference to witnesses’ testimony with: z) for the zomemin witness who testify falsely about an intended victim. m) for the second set of witnesses who are מזים - discredit the zomemim witnesses. The word hazamah does not appear in our text of the Gemara. Tosfos will defend the text that does have this word in this sentence. The word hazamah usually conjures the punishment meted out to zomemim z) witnesses whose testimony is discredited by other m) witnesses who testify that we were with the zomemim witnesses elsewhere at the time of the purported event and they could not possibly have seen the event about which they are testifying. Usually, zomemim witnesses are subject to the punishment for the crime that they are testifying about. This is not true when there is only one witness. This is of course the reason for not having the word hazamah in the text of our Gemara, since a single witness is not subject to this penalty. Tosfos explains that even though one witness is not subject to the standard b) punishment of zomemim witnesses, the word can appear in our Gemara. The word hazamah can rightfully appear here in this sentence, since by the process of hazamah [the single witness’] testimony is nullified. Even though the usual punishment for hazamah b) is inapplicable, the process does a) nullify his testimony. This is an inherent weakness of a single witness’ testimony as compared to a defendant’s admission. At this point in the dialogue, where the Gemara is demonstrating the relative strengths and weaknesses of various sources of information, it is justified in mentioning that the single witness’ testimony is subject to a) nullification by the process of hazamah. At this point the Gemara is attempting to disprove the kal v’chomer that witnesses’ testimony on half the plaintiff’s claim can obligate an oath by the defendant. The objective of such analysis is to show that one’s admission is inherently stronger than the testimony of witnesses and that is why one’s own admission generates an obligation to swear, whereas witnesses’ testimony which is inherently weaker cannot generate such an obligation. Proof that one’s admission is more powerful > than witnesses’ testimony: One’s own admission cannot be nullified by the hazamah process, whereas: Witnesses’ testimony can be nullified by the hazamah process. Tosfos now entertains the notion that there is an extremely powerful aspect of one’s own admission as compared to witnesses’ testimony that seems to have been overlooked by our Gemara. That is the fact that zomemim witnesses must pay the amount that they planned to obligate their victim to pay. For example: When z) witnesses testify that Ruvain owes Shimon one hundred zuz and it turns out that they are zomemim, the z) witnesses must pay Ruvain one hundred zuz. This is totally untrue of one’s own admission. For example: If Ruvain admits that he borrowed a hundred zuz from Shimom on Sunday the first of Nissan at twelve noon in New York City. m) Witnesses testify that Ruvain was with them in Los Angeles on Sunday the first of Nissan at nine a.m. Pacific time. Ruvain is not obligated to pay even though m) witnesses say that he falsely admitted that he owes Shimon one hundred zuz.2Tosfos seems to understand that it is because one’s own admission is so powerful that he is not required to pay even when discredited by m) witnesses. Some of the latter commentators raise the following question: Perhaps the reason Ruvain is not required to pay is because there is nobody to whom he must pay. After all, he himself was the intended victim of his false admission. To whom should he pay the hazamah penalty? See אמרי בנימין and נחלת משה who discuss this question. Perhaps, we are speaking of a situation where others will be hurt by his own admission, even though he is not directly testifying about them. For example: his debtors sold his loans to other people. If his debts are minimal they are worth significantly more than if he is heavily in debt. By falsely admitting that he owes much more money than he actually does, he is reducing the value of his paper and would be harming the holders of such loans. Tosfos assumes that he does not have to pay those loan holders the difference in value between when he had not yet admitted his greater liability and when he did falsely admit his greater liability. Perhaps the Gemara should have used this strength of one’s admission as compared to witnesses’ testimony to undermine the basis of the kal v’chomer. We should not ask this question to undermine the kal v’chomer by proving that one’s admission is stronger than witnesses’ testimony, as follows: What can you prove from one’s own admission, which is so powerful that he does not need to pay even when his admission is nullified by the process of hazamah? Can you say the same for witnesses who do need to pay their intended victim when their testimony is nullified by the process of hazamah? Proof that one’s admission is more powerful > than witnesses’ testimony: Witnesses must pay when their testimony is discredited by hazamah, whereas, One does not need to pay when his admission is discredited by hazamah. This challenge to the kal v’chomer is much more potent than the Gemara’s present challenge, which is demonstrating that one’s own admission stands even when assailed by hazamah as compared to the testimony of witnesses which is nullified by the process of hazamah. This is countered by introducing a solitary witness, whose testimony can also be nullified by hazamah and even so his testimony generates an oath obligation. Proof that even testimony that can be nullified by hazamah generates an oath obligation: The testimony of a single witness can be nullified by hazamah and even so it generates an oath obligation. But if the Gemara is demonstrating the power of an admission that he cannot be obligated to pay by the process of hazamah, we cannot use the single witness to prove that even when testimony is from a source that needs to pay when nullified by hazamah as proof that even testimony that is subject to the penalty of hazamah can generate an oath obligation, because the single witness also does not pay when his testimony is nullified by hazamah. Then had the Gemara focused on aspect b), the admission not being subject to paying when nullified by hazamah, [the Gemara] could not have said - a single witness proves that even testimony that is b) subject to pay when nullified by hazamah generates an oath obligation, because a single witness also does not pay when his testimony is nullified by hazamah. If so, both source of the common characteristic system would be instances where they cannot be obligated to pay via the process of hazamah. How can we learn from either of these two sources that witnesses’ testimony, which is subject to pay via the process of hazama can also generate an oath obligation? Tosfos explains that this argument that both sources of the kal v’chomer are beyond paying via hazamah is simply not true. It is definitely true that one’s admission is more powerful than witnesses’ testimony and that is why he does not pay when his admission is discredited by hazamah, but the same cannot be said for the single witness who does not pay when his testimony is nullified by two witnesses. For the reason that a single witness does not pay when his testimony is nullified by hazamah, is not because it is more stringent or more powerful than the testimony of two witnesses, as is one’s admission, rather, the reason for his not needing to pay is the inherent impotence of his testimony, because with his testimony he cannot obligate his intended victim to pay money. Therefore, since his statement is a) nullified4See Rashi חולין קטז. ד'ה אבל: We challenge common characteristic derivation with even an insignificant challenge. So are the systems of expounding the written law handed to us from Sinai. via the hazamah process, it is considered that his testimony is subject to hazamah. In the case of a single witness, the b) punishment aspect cannot be invoked, because of the inherent impotence of a single witness’ testimony, but the a) nullification aspect can be applied. Thus his testimony is viewed as subject to hazamah. Even so, he can generate an oath obligation, so too, two witnesses whose testimony is subject to hazamah can also generate an oath obligation. The Gemara will soon say: How can you learn from the common characteristic of an admission and a single witness, but in both those cases the punishment of hazamah is not applied? We see that the Gemara does consider aspect b) a proper challenge to the derivation. But shortly the Gemara does use this aspect b) to challenge the common characteristic derivation? That is because when dealing with a common characteristic derivation we may challenge it even with an insignificant challenge. There is a definite weakness in this challenge as Tosfos pointed out earlier. That a single witness does not pay when nullified by hazamah is not because of the strength of his testimony, but rather because of its weakness. The Rabanan consider this challenge as too weak to disprove a healthy kal v’chomer, but since ultimately both a single witness and one’s own admission are not subject to the penalty of hazamah, this can be used to negate a common characteristic derivation, even though it cannot be used to overturn a kal v’chomer.3See אוצר מפרשי התלמוד who quotes קצות החשן who discusses whether there is hazamah liability in an instance wherea solitary witness does cause a victim to pay. For example: In a situation where the defendant would have to swear, but cannot do so, he must pay. Would the zomem single witness be required to pay or not? See there for more discussion of this subject. R’ Chiya who does hold that we can learn from the common characteristic derivation that witnesses’ testimony can generate an oath obligation holds that even a common characteristic derivation, cannot be challenged with this insignificant challenge, because the reason that a single witness does not pay when nullified by hazamah, is because of his inherent weakness, because he did not have the power to generate a payment of money with his testimony.
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות defends the text reading ובהזמה for עד אחד, explaining that although a single witness does not pay damages when מוזם, the term is used because his testimony is nullified by ההזמה, and this nullification is not considered a חומרא in the context of the kal vachomer.

steps.title: מסקנא: Combined derivation
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara concludes that the derivation is from a combination of the admission and single witness cases, sharing the common denominator of claim and denial.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא concludes the derivation. It establishes that the obligation of an oath is derived from a combination of two sources, unified by a common denominator.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The final ruling (מסקנא) relies on a shared element between the source cases. This shared element validates the inclusion of the two-witness case in the derivation, resolving previous objections.

keyTerms.meaning: The derivation returns to its starting point with a refined understanding.
The common denominator that unifies the derivation.

steps.whatToRemember: The final derivation relies on the shared element of claim and denial.
steps.confusionAlert: The phrase 'וְחָזַר הַדִּין' indicates a return to the starting point with a refined understanding.
steps.whyThisMatters: It establishes the final legal basis for the obligation of an oath in the presence of witnesses.

phrases.aramaic: וְחָזַר הַדִּין: לֹא רְאִי זֶה כִּרְאִי זֶה,
phrases.english: And the derivation has reverted: this aspect is not like that aspect,
and that aspect is not like this aspect.

phrases.aramaic: הַצַּד הַשָּׁוֶה שֶׁבָּהֶן שֶׁעַל יְדֵי טַעֲנָה
phrases.english: The equal aspect that is in them, that comes by a claim
and denial they come, and he takes an oath.
Also I will bring witnesses,

phrases.aramaic: שֶׁעַל יְדֵי טַעֲנָה וּכְפִירָה הֵם בָּאִין – וְנִשְׁבָּע.
phrases.english: that come by a claim and denial – and he takes an oath.

rashi.hebrew: טענה וכפירה - זה טוען וזה כופר הן באין לדין:
rashi.english: A claim and a denial - [one litigant] claims and the other denies -- those which are coming to court.
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that טענה וכפירה refers to a standard monetary dispute where one party claims and the other denies, which is the prerequisite for an oath in court.

tosafot.hebrew: הצד השוה [שבהן] שע"י טענה כו' - וא"ת כופר הכל יוכיח שע"י טענה וכפירה פטור ויש לומר דבהצד השוה שבהן יש טענה חשובה דכשמודה מקצת או עד מכחישו נראה דמשקר ולכך יש שבועה:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות addresses the objection that a case of כופר הכל (denying everything) also involves טענה וכפירה but is exempt from an oath, and answers that the הצד השוה requires a significant טענה, such as מודה במקצת or witnesses contradicting the claim, which creates a presumption of lying and thus necessitates a שבועה.

meforshim.interplaySummary: תוספות raises a challenge to the Gemara's derivation by pointing out that כופר הכל also involves טענה וכפירה but is exempt, whereas רש״י simply defines the term without addressing this nuance; תוספות resolves this by refining the definition of the relevant טענה to cases where there is a partial admission or contradictory evidence, creating a stronger presumption of deceit.

steps.title: Challenge from status of כַּפָּרָן
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges the combined derivation: the common denominator cases do not establish the defendant as a confirmed liar, whereas witnesses do.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises an objection to the combined derivation. It points out that in the source cases, the defendant is not established as a confirmed liar, unlike in the two-witness case.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This objection attacks the derivation by highlighting a difference in the defendant's status. In the source cases, the defendant is not proven to be a liar. In the target case, he is proven to be a liar, which might undermine the derivation.

keyTerms.meaning: Established as a liar/denier.

steps.whatToRemember: The objection focuses on the defendant's status as a confirmed liar.
steps.confusionAlert: The term 'הוּחְזַק כַּפָּרָן' refers to being established as a liar.
steps.whyThisMatters: It tests the derivation by checking if the defendant's status affects the obligation.

phrases.aramaic: מָה לְהַצַּד הַשָּׁוֶה שֶׁבָּהֶן, שֶׁכֵּן לֹא הוּחְזַק כַּפְרָן.
phrases.english: What is notable about their common denominator, that the neighbor is not established as a liar.
Would you say [the same] with regard to witnesses, that the neighbor is established as…

steps.triggerLanguage: מָה לְהַצַּד הַשָּׁוֶה שֶׁבָּהֶן, שֶׁכֵּן לֹא הוּחְזַק כַּפְרָן

rashi.hebrew: לא הוחזק כפרן - על מה שכפר אינו מוחזק כפרן שמא לא כיחש שהעד אינו נאמן להכחישו ולהחזיקו כפרן:
rashi.english: Does not assume the presumptive status of one who [falsely] denies - he does not assume the presumptive status of a false denier because of what he denied -- maybe he has not [truly] contradicted him, since the [single] witness is not believed to render him [legally] contradicted and to cause him to assume the presumptive status of a [false] denier.
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that in the source cases (admission and single witness), the defendant is not established as a כפרן because a single witness is not believed to contradict him and establish that status.

rashi.hebrew: תאמר בעדים - שמאחר שכפר הכל והם העידו על החמשים הוחזק כפרן ולא נאמר נאמינהו על השבועה:
rashi.english: Would you say with regard to witnesses - due to the fact that he denied the entire [claim], and they testified about the fifty, he is presumed to be a [false] denier, and we shall not say that we will believe him if he takes an oath.
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that in the case of two witnesses, the defendant is established as a כפרן because he denied the entire claim while the witnesses testified to part of it, and therefore we would not believe him if he took an oath.

steps.title: Resolution via רַב חִסְדָּא's distinction
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the objection by citing Rav Hisda: denying a loan does not disqualify one as a liar, only denying a deposit does.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא answers the objection by citing רַב חִסְדָּא. רַב חִסְדָּא distinguishes between denying a loan and denying a deposit.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The resolution uses רַב חִסְדָּא's distinction to neutralize the objection. Since the case involves a loan, the defendant is not established as a confirmed liar. Thus, the difference highlighted in the objection does not apply.

keyTerms.meaning: Loan.
Deposit.
Amora who makes this distinction.

steps.whatToRemember: Denying a loan does not make one a confirmed liar, only denying a deposit does.
steps.confusionAlert: The distinction between מִלְוָה and פִּקָּדוֹן is crucial for determining liar status.
steps.whyThisMatters: It shows how specific legal distinctions can resolve objections to derivations.

phrases.english: And regarding witnesses, from where is he established as a liar!

phrases.aramaic: וְהָאָמַר רַב אִידִי בַּר אָבִין אָמַר רַב חִסְדָּא:
phrases.english: And Rav Idi bar Avin says Rav Hisda says:
One who denies a loan – is fit for testimony.
Regarding a deposit – is disqualified for testimony.

steps.triggerLanguage: וּבְעֵדִים מִי הוּחְזַק כַּפְרָן! וְהָאָמַר רַב אִידִי בַּר אָבִין אָמַר רַב חִסְדָּא

rashi.hebrew: הכופר במלוה - בין על כולה בין על מקצת ובאו עדים והעידוהו עד שלא נשבע:
rashi.english: One who denies a loan - whether the entire amount or a portion of it, and witnesses come and testify before he had a chance to swear.
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the status of כשר לעדות applies whether the defendant denied the entire loan or only a portion of it, provided witnesses testified against him before he was forced to take an oath.

rashi.hebrew: כשר לעדות - ולא אמרינן גזלן הוא והתורה אמרה (שמות כג) אל תשת רשע עד דכיון דמלוה להוצאה נתנה דלמא צריך להוציאה וסבר עד דהוה לי [זוזי] ופרענא ליה ואי מודינא ליה השתא יתבעני מיד אבל הכופר בפקדון אפילו על מקצת ובאו עדים והעידוהו פסול לעדות דמאי הוה ליה גבי פקדון לאשתמוטי נפשיה ושמא תאמר אבד ממנו ואשתמיט ליה עד דמשכח ליה לקמן בפירקין (בבא מציעא דף ה:) מוקמינן בדאתו סהדי ואמרי ההיא שעתא בידיה נקיט ליה:
rashi.english: Fit to bear witness - and we do not say he is a thief. The Torah states: "you shall not join hands with the guilty to act as a witness." [Exodus 23:1] Since loaned [money] is meant to be spent, maybe he needed to spend it, and thought "if I admit to him now, before I have the money, he will demand it from me immediately." But one who denies receiving a deposit, or even a portion thereof, and witnesses come and testify against him, he is rendered unfit to give testimony -- what right did he have to evade [the owner]? Perhaps you might say that he lost [the entrusted item] and is evading [the owner] until he can find it -- later on in our chapter we will establish that [we are dealing with a situation] where witnesses came and testified that [the bailee] has it in his possession right now.
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that a person who denies a מלוה is not considered a גזלן because the money was given to be spent, so he might have legitimately used it and is merely delaying repayment until he has funds, whereas denying a פיקדון renders one פסול לעדות because there is no valid excuse for withholding an item that was never meant to be used.

steps.title: Challenge from הַזָּמָה
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara raises a new objection: the common denominator cases are not subject to Hazamah, whereas witnesses are.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a new objection to the combined derivation. It points out that the source cases are not subject to the laws of הַזָּמָה, unlike the two-witness case.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This objection attacks the derivation by highlighting a difference in legal vulnerability. The source cases are not subject to הַזָּמָה, while the target case is. This difference might undermine the derivation.

keyTerms.meaning: Laws of conspiracy punishment.

steps.whatToRemember: The objection focuses on the applicability of הַזָּמָה laws.
steps.confusionAlert: הַזָּמָה refers to the punishment for conspiring witnesses.
steps.whyThisMatters: It tests the derivation by checking if the source and target cases share the same legal risks.

phrases.english: But refute it like this:
What is notable about their common denominator —
they are not subject to the law of conspiring witnesses,
you would say about witnesses who are subject to the law of conspiring witnesses!

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא פָּרֵךְ הָכִי: מָה לְהַצַּד הַשָּׁוֶה שֶׁבָּהֶן – שֶׁכֵּן אֵינָן בְּתוֹרַת הֲזָמָה

rashi.hebrew: בתורת הזמה - לשלם קנס דכאשר זמם דעד אחד שהוזם פטור ופיו גבי הודאה כל שכן דלא שייכא הזמה כלל:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the phrase בתורת הזמה refers to the obligation to pay a fine when witnesses are convicted of conspiracy, noting that a single witness is exempt from this punishment, and an admission is even more clearly not subject to it.

rashi.hebrew: תאמר בעדים - אם הוזמו בחמשים שהעידו משלמין ממון:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the term בעדים refers to two witnesses who, if convicted of conspiracy regarding a testimony of fifty dinars, are liable to pay monetary compensation.

steps.title: Resolution: רַבִּי חִיָּה does not object from הַזָּמָה
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara resolves the objection by stating that Rabbi Chiya does not object based on Hazamah, as it does not apply to the admission case.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא answers the objection by stating that רַבִּי חִיָּה does not raise an objection based on הַזָּמָה. This is because הַזָּמָה does not apply to the admission case at all.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The resolution dismisses the objection by noting that הַזָּמָה is irrelevant to the admission case. Since the source case is not subject to הַזָּמָה, the difference highlighted in the objection does not undermine the derivation.

keyTerms.meaning: Does not refute/objection.

steps.whatToRemember: רַבִּי חִיָּה does not object from הַזָּמָה because it doesn't apply to admission.
steps.confusionAlert: The resolution relies on the inapplicability of הַזָּמָה to the source case.
steps.whyThisMatters: It shows how the irrelevance of a legal concept to the source case can neutralize an objection.

phrases.english: This is not difficult:

phrases.aramaic: רַבִּי חִיָּיא תּוֹרַת הֲזָמָה לָא פָּרֵיךְ.
phrases.english: Rabbi Ḥiyya does not derive a fortiori inference.

steps.triggerLanguage: הָא לָא קַשְׁיָא: רַבִּי חִיָּיא תּוֹרַת הֲזָמָה לָא פָּרֵיךְ

rashi.hebrew: תורת הזמה לא פריך - אינה חשובה לו פירכא דכיון דעד אחד ישנו בהזמה ליבטל עדותו בעדות שני עדים אם הוה לן למימר בעדים משום דישנן בהזמה לא יחייבוהו שבועה הוה לן למימר נמי בעד אחד לא יחייבהו שבועה דהא ישנו בהזמה ליבטל עדותו ואי משום דאין משלם קנס אין בזו יפוי כח להאמין עדותו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבי חייא does not object from תורת הַזָּמָה because if the presence of הַזָּמָה in witnesses would exempt them from a שְׁבוּעָה, then a single witness subject to הַזָּמָה should also be exempt, but since a single witness does not pay קנס, there is no stronger reason to believe his testimony in this case.

steps.title: Question about similarity to מִשְׁנָה
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks how Rabbi Chiya's case is similar to the Mishna's case, given the difference in witness distribution.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a question about the similarity between רַבִּי חִיָּה's case and the מִשְׁנָה's case. In the מִשְׁנָה, the creditor has witnesses but the debtor does not. In רַבִּי חִיָּה's case, both parties have witnesses.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This question challenges the applicability of the מִשְׁנָה's ruling to רַבִּי חִיָּה's case. The difference in witness distribution might affect the legal outcome, raising doubts about the comparison.

keyTerms.meaning: What is similar?
Witnesses.

steps.whatToRemember: The question focuses on the difference in witness distribution between the two cases.
steps.confusionAlert: The distinction between 'לְמַלְוֵה אִית לֵהּ סַהֲדִי' and 'לְלוֵה לֵית לֵהּ סַהֲדִי' is key.
steps.whyThisMatters: It tests the validity of comparing two cases with different factual setups.

phrases.aramaic: אֶלָּא דְּקָאָמַר וְתַנָּא תּוּנָא מִי דָּמֵי?
phrases.english: Rather, he says: And the tanna also taught a similar halakha?
There, the lender has witnesses,

phrases.aramaic: לְלֹוֶה לֵית לֵיהּ סָהֲדֵי דְּלָא מַסֵּיק
phrases.english: the borrower does not have witnesses that he does not bring
it to him and does not demand payment,

phrases.aramaic: דְּאִי הֲווֹ לֵיהּ סָהֲדֵי לְלֹוֶה דְּלָא
phrases.english: for if he had witnesses that the borrower does not
bring it to him and does not demand payment –
Rabbi Ḥiyya would not require him to take an oath,

phrases.aramaic: הָכָא כִּי הֵיכִי דַּאֲנַן סָהֲדֵי בְּהַאי –
phrases.english: Here, just as we are witnesses to this –
we are witnesses to this,
and even so he is required to take an oath!

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא דְּקָאָמַר וְתַנָּא תּוּנָא מִי דָּמֵי?

rashi.hebrew: אלא דקאמר ותנא תונא - אלא אם יש לך להשיב על דברי רבי חייא כך יש לך להשיב דקאמר ותנא תונא והביא ראיה לדבריו ממשנתינו מי דמי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara's question of 'מי דמי' is a challenge to the proof from the Mishna, asking how the two cases can be similar.

rashi.hebrew: התם - בדרבי חייא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that 'התם' refers to the case of רבי חייא.

rashi.hebrew: למלוה אית ליה סהדי - דמסיק ביה חמשים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the creditor in רבי חייא's case has witnesses for the fifty dinars he claims are owed.

rashi.hebrew: ללוה לית ליה סהדי - באינך חמשים שאינו חייב לו עליה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the debtor lacks witnesses for the remaining fifty dinars he claims he does not owe.

rashi.hebrew: אי הוה ליה כו' - כגון אילו העידו העדים חמשים חייב וחמשים פרע בכה"ג לא הוה אמר ר' חייא דצריך לישבע על השאר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that if the debtor had witnesses for his partial payment, רבי חייא would not require him to take a שבועה for the remainder.

rashi.hebrew: מתני' כי היכי דאנן סהדי להאי - דחציה שלו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that in the Mishna, the witnesses testify that half of the disputed amount belongs to the debtor.

rashi.hebrew: אנן סהדי להאי - דחציה שלו ולמה הצריכוהו חכמים שבועה ש"מ טעמא לאו משום מודה מקצת הטענה הוא כר' חייא ואינה שבועה דאורייתא אלא תקנת חכמים שלא יהא כל אחד הולך כו':
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that since the debtor in the Mishna has witnesses for half the claim yet still requires a שבועה, the reason for the oath cannot be מודה מקצת, but rather a rabbinic enactment to prevent people from claiming everything.

steps.title: רַבִּי חִיָּה's ruling on הֵילָךְ
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara clarifies that the earlier teaching about 'הֵילָךְ' (here it is) refers to the opinion of רבי חייא, who rules that a defendant who admits to part of a debt and hands over the admitted amount while saying 'הֵילָךְ' is still obligated to take an oath regarding the remainder.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא introduces the alternative opinion of רַבִּי חִיָּה regarding the phrase 'הֵילָךְ' and explains his reasoning.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא clarifies that the earlier teaching about 'הֵילָךְ' refers to the opinion of רַבִּי חִיָּה. רַבִּי חִיָּה rules that a defendant who admits to part of a debt and hands over the admitted amount while saying 'הֵילָךְ' is still obligated to take an oath regarding the remainder.

keyTerms.meaning: A Tannaic sage whose opinion is being discussed.
The phrase 'here it is,' used when handing over money.
Partial admission of a claim, which triggers an oath.
Oath, specifically the oath required in cases of partial admission.

steps.whatToRemember: רַבִּי חִיָּה considers 'הֵילָךְ' as equivalent to a partial admission, thus obligating an oath.
steps.confusionAlert: The phrase 'הֵילָךְ' might seem to imply a full admission, but רַבִּי חִיָּה treats it as a partial admission because the defendant is only admitting to part of the claim.
steps.whyThisMatters: This ruling establishes the legal status of 'הֵילָךְ' in the context of partial admissions and oaths.

phrases.english: Rather, when it was stated: "And the tanna of the mishna also taught"
with regard to that which was stated by Rabbi Ḥiyya,
that Rabbi Ḥiyya says:
"I have a maneh in your possession" and the other says:
"You have in my possession only fifty zuz,
and here you are" — he is obligated.
What is the reason?
"Here you are" is also like an admission of part of the claim.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֶלָּא כִּי אִיתְּמַר ״וְתַנָּא תּוּנָא״ – אַאִידַּךְ דְּרַבִּי חִיָּיא אִיתְּמַר, דְּאָמַר רַבִּי חִיָּיא: ״מָנֶה לִי בְּיָדְךָ״ וְהַלָּה אוֹמֵר: ״אֵין לְךָ בְּיָדִי אֶלָּא חֲמִישִּׁים זוּז, וְהֵילָךְ״ – חַיָּיב. מַאי טַעְמָא – ״הֵילָךְ״ נָמֵי כְּמוֹדֶה מִקְצָת הַטְּעָנָה דָּמֵי.

rashi.hebrew: והילך - לא הוצאתים והן שלך בכ"מ שהם:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the phrase הֵילָךְ means 'I have not spent your money, it remains yours wherever it is,' indicating the defendant is returning the funds without admitting ownership.

rashi.hebrew: חייב - לישבע על השאר ולא אמרינן הני דקמודי ליה בגוייהו הואיל ואיתנהו בעינייהו כמאן דנקיט להו דמי אלא כשאר מודה במקצת הוא וחייב:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the defendant is חַיָּיב to take a שבועה on the remainder because handing over the money does not exempt him, as it is treated like a standard מודה במקצת case.

tosafot.hebrew: ותנא תונא אאידך דר' חייא - אנן סהדי דמאי דתפיס האי כו' פרש"י כיון דהוי כהעדאת עדים משתבע מדרבי חייא קמייתא וקשה כיון דלא מצי לאוכוחי הך ברייתא דהילך אלא מכח קמייתא לימא ותנא תונא אקמייתא לכך י"ל דאנן סהדי לאו דוקא דמאי דתפיס מחשבינן ליה השתא טפי מהעדאת עדים וחשיב כאילו מודה ליה דדידיה הוא אך קשה דמ"מ איכא למיפרך דמתני' כי היכי דהאי מודה להאי האי נמי מודה להאי ואפ"ה קמשתבעי כדפריך אקמייתא ומאי תיקן וי"ל דהשתא נמי מוכח מכח טעמא דבסמוך אי הילך פטור לא הוי מתקני רבנן שבועה דליכא דכוותה באורייתא אבל לרבי חייא קמייתא כי נמי בהעדאת עדים פטור מתקני רבנן שבועה במתני' דחשיב הודאה במאי דתפיס חבריה ודוחק דלפ"ז הא דלא קאמר ותנא תונא אקמייתא משום דמאי דתפיס חשיב כהודאה וזו לא היתה פירכתו למעלה אלא מטעם כי היכי דאנן סהדי להאי כו':
tosafot.english: “The Tanna [of our Mishna] taught this”, [refers to] another [rule taught] by R’ Chiya. A review of our Gemara is in place: On 3a R’ Chiya teaches that A) if a plaintiff claims one hundred zuz and the defendant denies the claim entirely and two witnesses testify that the defendant owes fifty zuz, the defendant must swear that he does not owe the second fifty zuz, just as the defendant would need to swear if he had admitted owing fifty zuz. This ruling is referred to as the earlier teaching of R’ Chiya. Initially the Gemara said that ‘our Tanna taught’ this ruling in our Mishna. The Gemara on 4a then showed how this ruling cannot be deduced from our Mishna. The Gemara continues saying that there is a second ruling of R’ Chiya that our Tanna taught in the Mishna. That is the rule of B) ‘הילך - here, it is yours’. For example: Ruvain says to Shimon you owe me one hundred zuz. Shimon responds: I owe you only fifty and here, it is yours. Shimon offers immediate payment for the fifty zuz that he admits owing. R’ Chiya says that even in this case Shimon needs to swear. Rav Sheishes disagrees. He holds that one swears only when he does not immediately offer to pay the portion he owes. The Gemara is now saying that when R’ Chiya said ‘the Tanna taught’ my ruling in the Mishna, he was referring to B) this latter ruling and not to A) his earlier ruling. In order to view this ruling and how it can be deduced from our Mishna from the proper perspective, we must understand that the case of the Mishna is one where there is an oath obligation similar to a) one who partially admits the claim of the plaintiff and b) he is obligated to swear even though he is offering immediate payment for the portion he owes. We must first see how in our Mishna a) there is an oath obligation similar to when one admits partial liability and then we can perhaps see R’ Chiya’s second ruling: b) that even when the defendant offers immediate payment, he is obligated to swear. For greater clarity let us review Rashi together: The Gemara initially thought that according to R’ Chiya, the reason for our Mishna’s ruling is because, we [the court] testify that the portion of the garment that each of the litigants is holding is his. This is similar to when one claims: you owe me one hundred zuz and the other responds: I owe you nothing. While witnesses testify that [the defendant] owes fifty zuz, as in the other [earlier ruling] of R’ Chiya, where [R’ Chiya] obligates [the defendant] to take a Torah oath. We will look at this case first from Ruvain’s and then from Shimon’s perspective: Ruvain claims that the garment is entirely mine. This is the equivalent of saying that you Shimon, owe me one hundred zuz. Shimon claims: it is entirely mine. This is the equivalent of Shimon saying: I owe you nothing! The court sees that half is definitely Ruvain’s, because he is holding the garment. This the equivalent of witnesses testifying that half of Ruvain’s claim is true. The Mishna rules that Shimon must swear to defend the half of the garment that he does receive. This is similar to R’ Chiya’s first ruling that the testimony of witnesses accomplishes the same as one’s own admission. Now, if we just reverse the roles of Ruvain and Shimon, we will have the same exact ruling for Shimon as we do for Ruvain. The Gemara sees this ruling of the Mishna in this light. Thus we have in our Mishna the basis for an oath obligation a) R’ Chiya’s first ruling. The Gemara also sees this case as one of ‘הילך - Here, it is yours!’, since delivery of half the garment to each of the litigants is immediate. Thus we have demonstrated R’ Chiya’s second ruling b) that even in a case of ‘הילך - Here, it is yours!’, there is an oath obligation. We, the court, testify that which this etc. [litigant is holding is his, and as if the other litigant said , “Here, it is yours.”1The words אנן סהדי in the Gemara are in brackets. It appears that at some point they were added to the text. They do appear in Rashi’s explanation of the Gemara. It is unclear whether Rashi had those words in his text of the Gemara. It is interesting to note that Tosfos wrestles with the meaning of these words as they pertain to our Gemara. He does not mention that there is an alternative text. See Artscroll’s translation of the text and note 28. Rashi explains that since our perception of what we see, is that Ruvain and Shimon each own at least half of the garment, this is equivalent to the testimony of witnesses who testify that Shimon owes Ruvain half of Ruvain’s claim. Shimon must swear that at least half is his because of R’ Chiya’s earlier ruling, that the testimony of witnesses (in this case, the court’s perception that Ruvain owns at least half of the garment) can generate an oath obligation on the other half of the garment. As explained earlier, we must first have an oath obligation and then we can apply R’ Chiya’s second ruling that even when immediate payment is offered the defendant is also obligated to take an oath. According to Rashi’s explanation, the basis for the oath obligation in our Mishna is R’ Chiya’s earlier ruling. Thus, by demonstrating that the Mishna is teaching R’ Chiya’s second ruling, we automatically have proof of his first ruling. If not for the first ruling, there would be no oath obligation at all. This is difficult, for since we cannot prove this latter2We have corrected the text to conform with רש"ש. ruling b) about when the defendant says, הילך - Here, it is yours!’, only because of the earlier ruling of R’ Chiya a) that one is obligated to swear upon the testimony of witnesses who say that the defendant is partially liable, we should also say ‘the Tanna taught’ about the earlier ruling of R’ Chiya. Since we can prove ruling b) only if ruling a) is true, any proof of ruling b) is automatically proof of ruling a).3See ריטב"א רמב"ן, and ר"ן who offer a defense of Rashi. In general their explanation is that when the Gemara said “our Tanna taught it” in reference to the latter ruling of R’ Chiya, the Gemara was referring to the conclusion of the Gemara later where R’ Chiya admits that the oath in our Mishna is of Rabbinic origin. Thus, we have no proof of R’ Chiya’s earlier ruling. However, when the Gemara initially begins its conversation about proving R’ Chiya’s second ruling from our Mishna because the oath in our Mishna is required by Torah law, the Gemara could have in fact used the very same proof to prove R’ Chiya’s earlier ruling. R’ Chiya ultimately admits that the oath in our Mishna must be of Rabbinic origin and at that point we no longer have proof of R’ Chiya’s earlier ruling, but we can prove his latter ruling, for if ‘Here, it is yours!’ is exempt from an oath by Torah law, it would also be exempt in Rabbinic law. בעל המאור quoted in ,שיטה מקובצת offers a different approach. We are compelled to say that it is possible that ruling b) can exist without ruling a), and that our Mishna need not be understood specifically as a case of b) R’ Chiya’s latter ruling being imposed on an oath obligation established by his a) earlier ruling. There must be a way of explaining the initial oath obligation other then as a result of R’ Chiya’s earlier ruling. We already know that a) one’s partial admission generates a Torah oath obligation. We are considering that b) the testimony of witnesses about part of the plaintiff’s claim might also generate a Torah oath obligation. R’ Chiya’s latter ruling that even when the defendant says, “Here, it is yours!”, he must swear by Torah law is applicable in both instances, when the original claim is a) partially admitted by the defendant or b) when we hear from witnesses that he owes part of the claim. Our Gemara seems to be saying4See note 1. In Shulchan Aruch C.M. 75, 4, we do in fact rule like the earlier ruling of R’ Chiya that when liability of fifty zuz is established by witnesses, the defendant must swear by Torah law. that the basis for the oath in our Mishna is that the court who sees that half of the garment belongs to Ruvain is the equivalent of b) the ' testimony. But must we say that the court’s view of this situation is equivalent to b) witnesses’ testimony? Perhaps it is equivalent to hearing an admission from Shimon that half the garment belongs to Ruvain? For this reason we can say, that the words in brackets in our text ‘we, the court, are witnesses’ used to describe our perception that Shimon owes Ruvain half the garment is imprecise, rather, now we consider that which [Ruvain] is holding [the garment] even more than the testimony of witnesses. It is considered as if [Shimon] admits to [Ruvain] that [half] is [Ruvain’s]. Thus the Mishna is an example of when R’ Chiya’s latter ruling is imposed on a) one’s own partial admission. There is no evidence at all that witnesses’ testimony that Ruvain owns half the garment would generate a Torah oath. Tosfos will now show that his explanation of the Gemara’s phrase ‘אנן סהדי - we are witnesses’ as meaning ‘we perceive an admission’ is not problem free. As usual, we will need to review the Gemara’s rejection of R’ Chiya’s earlier ruling. The Gemara is discussing whether R’ Chiya’s earlier ruling was ‘taught by the Tanna’ of our Mishna: But that which [R’ Chiya] said “and our Tanna taught this” in our Mishna, is it comparable? There, in R’ Chiya’s case, the lender has witnesses that the borrower borrowed fifty zuz, but the borrower has no witnesses that he does not owe the lender more than the fifty zuz that the witnesses testified about. For if the borrower had witnesses that he does not owe the lender anymore, R’ Chiya would not require an oath for the balance, since the borrower has witnesses who testify that he owes no more than fifty zuz. Here, in the case of our Mishna, just as we are witnesses that [Ruvain] owns half, we are also witnesses that [Shimon] owns half, and even so an oath is required! It seems that the Gemara’s rejection of R’ Chiya’s earlier ruling is because whatever evidence we have on behalf of Ruvain, we also have that very same evidence on behalf of Shimon. Just as we have the equivalent of witnesses testifying that Ruvain owns half, we have witnesses who testify that Shimon owns half. When discussing R’ Chiya’s latter ruling, the Gemara neglects to cite the very same difficulty. Tosfos will now re-examine the basis for the oath in our Mishna, before we reach the issue of ‘Here, it is yours! But this remains difficult! For in any case we can challenge R’ Chiya’s deduction of his latter ruling from our Mishna by arguing that in our Mishna, just as [Shimon] admits to [Ruvain] that half is his, so too, [Ruvain] is admitting to [Shimon] that half is his and even so an oath is required, as [the Gemara] asks this very question about R’ Chiya’s earlier ruling. If so, how is this attempt to say that R’ Chiya was not discussing his first ruling but his latter ruling an improvement over the previous discussion about the earlier ruling of R’ Chiya? The same problem we have with the earlier ruling re-emerges with the latter ruling. Eventually, our Gemara says that even if the oath obligation in our Mishna is inherently Rabbinic, the Rabanan would not institute an oath that is not at all similar to an oath required by Torah law. Thus, if in a case of ‘הילך - Here, it is yours!’ there would be no obligation to swear by Torah law, the Rabanan would not institute a Rabbinic oath in a similar situation. Tosfos suggest that even at this earlier point in the dialogue, the Gemara was utilizing this approach, even though it is first articulated later in the Gemara. We can answer: That now in this earlier point in the dialogue as well [the Gemara] is proving that in a case of ‘הילך - Here, it is yours!’ there is an oath obligation because of the reason that will soon be mentioned in the Gemara: If when a defendant who has admitted half says ‘Here, it is yours!’, he is exempt from an oath by Torah law, the Rabanan would not have instituted a Rabbinic oath that is not comparable to any oath prescribed by the Torah. Thus, the fact that in our Mishna an oath is required, even if this happens to be a situation where the oath is only Rabbinic, because the evidence on both sides is equal,it still proves that by Torah law in a situation of ‘Here, it is yours!’, there is an obligation to swear. We cannot say the same for R’ Chiya’s earlier ruling. It is possible that even if the testimony of witnesses cannot generate an oath obligation, the litigants in our Mishna would still be obligated to swear. However, according to the earlier ruling of R’ Chiya, even if witnesses’ testimony cannot generate an oath obligation and when witnesses testify that the defendant owes half the plaintiff’s claim he is exempt from an oath, the Rabanan would institute an oath in the case of our Mishna, because the fact that the litigants are each holding the garment is tantamount to an admission, which certainly generates an oath obligation. When we consider the source of information in our Mishna, which is that we see the two litigants each holding the garment, we may view this either as witnesses’ testimony or as an admission that half the garment belongs to each of the litigants. Even if we reject R’ Chiya’s earlier ruling that the testimony of witnesses generates an oath obligation, the case of two litigants holding a garment requires an oath because the fact that they are holding the garment is tantamount to an admission. Thus, the Gemara is correct in its rejection of R’ Chiya’s earlier ruling. However, if when a litigant says, “Here, it is yours!,’ he is exempt from taking an oath, there could be no requirement for an oath in our Mishna, because our Mishna is clearly a case of ‘Here, it is yours!’. Although Tosfos is satisfied with the logic of his explanation of the Gemara, he is unhappy with how it conforms to the text of our Gemara. This explanation is awkward! For according to this explanation, the reason that [the Gemara] could not say “and the Tanna taught it” in reference to the earlier ruling of R’ Chiya is because we can view [the litigants’] holding the garment as an admission and not as witnesses’ testimony, but that was not the gist of[the Gemara’s] challenge earlier. Rather, the reason for saying that R’ Chiya’s earlier ruling is not taught by the Tanna of our Mishna was articulated as: because just as we are witnesses for [Ruvain], we are also witnesses for [Shimon] etc., but according to Tosfos’ explanation that is not the reason at all, it is because each litigant’s holding the garment can be viewed as an admission by his opponent.
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that רבי חייא's statement 'וְתַנָּא תּוּנָא' refers to this second ruling on הֵילָךְ rather than his earlier ruling on witness testimony, because the Mishna's case involves an obligation to swear even when the defendant offers immediate payment, which aligns with the הֵילָךְ scenario.

steps.title: Proof from the מִשְׁנָה of two holding a garment
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara cites the Mishnah of two people holding a garment as a parallel case to support רבי חייא's ruling that 'הֵילָךְ' obligates an oath.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא brings a proof from the מִשְׁנָה of two people holding a garment to support רַבִּי חִיָּה's ruling.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא cites the מִשְׁנָה of two people holding a garment as a parallel case to support רַבִּי חִיָּה's ruling. In that מִשְׁנָה, each person holds half the garment, and the גמרא explains that this is similar to saying 'הֵילָךְ' because each person is in possession of what they hold.

keyTerms.meaning: The מִשְׁנָה of two people holding a garment.
To grasp or hold, indicating possession.
He must take an oath.

steps.whatToRemember: The מִשְׁנָה of two holding a garment is used as a parallel case to support רַבִּי חִיָּה's ruling.
steps.confusionAlert: The connection between the two cases might not be immediately obvious, but the גמרא explains that both involve partial possession and partial admission.
steps.whyThisMatters: This proof strengthens רַבִּי חִיָּה's position by showing a similar case in the מִשְׁנָה.

phrases.english: And the tanna of the mishna taught: Two holding a tallit.
And here, since he grasps –

phrases.aramaic: אֲנַן סָהֲדֵי דְּמַאי דְּתָפֵיס הֵילָךְ הוּא, וְקָתָנֵי: יִשָּׁבַע.
phrases.english: We are witnesses that what he grasps is his, and it teaches: Let him take an oath.

steps.triggerLanguage: וְתַנָּא תּוּנָא: שְׁנַיִם אוֹחֲזִין בְּטַלִּית. וְהָא הָכָא, כֵּיוָן דְּתָפֵיס – אֲנַן סָהֲדֵי דְּמַאי דְּתָפֵיס הֵילָךְ הוּא, וְקָתָנֵי: יִשָּׁבַע.

rashi.hebrew: ותנא תונא כו' - קס"ד דלר' חייא טעמא דמתני' משום דאנן סהדי דמאי דתפיס דיליה הוא והוי כמנה לי בידך והלה אמר אין לך בידי כלום והעדים מעידים אותו שיש לו חמשים וכי אידך דרבי חייא דמחייב ליה שבועה דאורייתא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the Gemara assumes רבי חייא's reasoning for the Mishnah is that since we are witnesses that what each person holds belongs to them, it is like a case of 'מנה לי בידך' where witnesses testify to the debt, thus obligating a biblical שבועה.

rashi.hebrew: והא הכא - דהך העדאת עדים אנן סהדי דמאי דתפיס האי הילך הוא שהרי בפנינו הוא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the phrase 'אנן סהדי' refers to the fact that we are witnesses that what each person is holding is indeed theirs, as they are holding it in front of us.

rashi.hebrew: וקתני - דמחייב שבועה אשארא וה"ה להודאה במקצת:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י notes that the Mishnah obligates a שבועה on the remainder of the claim, and this principle equally applies to a case of הודאה במקצת.

steps.title: רַב שֵׁשֶׁת's View on הֵילָךְ
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara introduces רַב שֵׁשֶׁת's opposing view that saying 'הֵילָךְ' exempts the defendant from an oath, as the admitted amount is considered already in the creditor's possession.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara introduces רַב שֵׁשֶׁת's opposing view on the legal status of הֵילָךְ. This is a setup line presenting a new opinion.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַב שֵׁשֶׁת rules that saying הֵילָךְ exempts the defendant from an oath. He reasons that handing over the admitted amount means the creditor already possesses those funds, so no partial admission remains to trigger an oath.

keyTerms.meaning: An Amoraic sage whose opinion is being discussed.
Exempt, in this case from taking an oath.
To hold or possess, indicating that the creditor already has the money.

steps.whatToRemember: רַב שֵׁשֶׁת considers הֵילָךְ as transferring possession, thus exempting from an oath.
steps.confusionAlert: The difference between רַב חִיָּה and רַב שֵׁשֶׁת lies in whether הֵילָךְ is seen as a partial admission or as transferring possession.
steps.whyThisMatters: This opposing view sets up the central dispute in the sugya regarding the legal status of הֵילָךְ.

phrases.english: And Rav Sheshet says: 'Here you are' — exempt.
What is the reason? Since he said to him 'Here you are',
these two dinars that he is admitting to them —
like one who holds them as a loan of money.
In the other fifty, he does not admit,
therefore there is no admission of part of the claim.

steps.triggerLanguage: וְרַב שֵׁשֶׁת אָמַר ״הֵילָךְ פָּטוּר״. מַאי טַעְמָא? כֵּיוָן דְּאָמַר לֵיהּ ״הֵילָךְ״, הָנֵי זוּזִי דְּקָא מוֹדֵי בְּגַוַּיְיהוּ – כְּמַאן דְּנָקֵיט לְהוּ מַלְוֶה דָּמֵי. בְּאִינָךְ חֲמִשִּׁים הָא לָא מוֹדֵי, הִלְכָּךְ לֵיכָּא הוֹדָאַת מִקְצָת הַטְּעָנָה.

tosafot.hebrew: ורב ששת אמר הילך פטור - נראה שהלכה כרב ששת דבפ' השואל (לקמן בבא מציעא דף ק.) גבי עבד קטן פריך ועוד הילך הוא אך בספר חפץ משמע דהילך חייב [וע' תוס' ב"ב קכח: ד"ה הלכתא]:
tosafot.english: And Rav Sheshet says: [One who says]: Here you are [is] exempt. Our Gemara fails to rule in this dispute. Tosfos tells us that elsewhere the Gemara does demonstrate its halachic preference in this issue. It appears that the halacha is in accordance with Rav Sheishes, for in Perek Hashoel (below 100a) in regard to a dispute about whether a large slave or a small slave was sold,1A seller sold one of two slaves: a smaller or a larger slave. They are now disputing which of the two slaves was sold. The Mishna there rules that the seller must swear that he sold a small slave. The Gemara challenges this ruling for three reasons: a) The claim is not about the same item that he admitted. b) It is a case of “Here, it is yours!, and c) one does not swear about slaves. Since the Gemara uses the argument that it is b) a case of “Here, it is yours!” it is proof that the Gemara assumes that the halacha follows Rav Sheishes. [the Gemara] argues that there should be no oath obligation because it is a case of “Here, it is yours!”.2Shulchan Aruch Choshen Mishpat 75, 6, also rules that in a case of “Here, it is yours!” the defendant is exempt from a Torah oath. He is however required to take a Rabbinic oath, called שבועת היסת, which will be discussed later. The Gemara there is a clear indication that it assumes that one is exempt from swearing in a case oh “Here, it is yours!”. However, in Sefer Chaifetz it is to be understood that he rules that in a case of “Here, it is yours” the defendant is obligated to swear.3See רא"ש who presents some difficulties with the ruling that in a case of “Here, it is yours!” one is exempt from a Torah oath. The רא"ש manage to find differences betyween the cases that he quotes and the Gemara in Hashoel 100a. Perhaps Sefer Hacheifets feels that those Gemaros are in fact proof that the halacha should follow R’ Chiya. See Tosfos Bava Basra 128b: ד'ה הלכתא. Tosfos there quotes Rabbeinu Chananel4See – מהר"ץ חיות quoted in the digest of ש"ס נהרדעא who explains that there are those who held that Sefer Hacheifetz was written by Rabbeinu Chananel. The autyhor of this gloss is disproving thios theory by showing that Rabbeinu Chananel rules that ‘הילך’ is exempt from a Torah oath in opposition to Sefer Hacheifetz. as also ruling in accordance with Rav Sheishes that in a case of “Here, it is yours!” the defendant is exempt from swearing.
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות rules that the הלכה follows רב ששת, based on a proof from בבא מציעא ק. that uses הילך to exempt an oath, though they note that ספר חפץ rules the opposite.

steps.title: Challenge to רַב שֵׁשֶׁת from the Mishnah
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges רַב שֵׁשֶׁת's view by pointing out that the Mishnah requires an oath in a similar case, which contradicts his ruling.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara raises a challenge to רַב שֵׁשֶׁת's view based on the Mishnah. This is a direct objection.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara points out that the Mishnah requires an oath in a similar case, contradicting רַב שֵׁשֶׁת's ruling. The Mishnah of two holding a garment requires an oath, which seems to support רַב חִיָּה's view that הֵילָךְ obligates an oath.

keyTerms.meaning: A challenge or objection.
The Mishnah, specifically the one about two holding a garment.

steps.whatToRemember: The Mishnah's requirement of an oath challenges רַב שֵׁשֶׁת's view.
steps.confusionAlert: The challenge is based on the apparent contradiction between the Mishnah and רַב שֵׁשֶׁת's ruling.
steps.whyThisMatters: This challenge forces רַב שֵׁשֶׁת to provide a resolution to reconcile his view with the Mishnah.

phrases.english: And according to Rav Sheshet, the Mishna is difficult!

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר לְךָ רַב שֵׁשֶׁת: מַתְנִיתִין תַּקָּנַת חֲכָמִים הִיא.
phrases.english: Rav Sheshet says to you: The Mishna is a rabbinic ordinance.

rashi.hebrew: תקנת חכמים היא - ולא משום העדאת עדים וכדפרכינן לעיל כי היכי דאנן סהדי להאי אנן סהדי להאי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רַב שֵׁשֶׁת resolves the contradiction by stating the Mishnah's requirement for an oath is a תקנת חכמים, not derived from the principle of העדאת עדים (as the Gemara previously challenged).

steps.title: How would רַב חִיָּה respond to the rabbinic enactment?
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara asks how רבי חייא would respond to the claim that the Mishnah's oath is a rabbinic enactment, questioning whether the Sages would institute an oath without a Torah-level basis.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara raises a question about how רַב חִיָּה would respond to the resolution. This is a setup line for a potential counter-argument.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara asks how רַב חִיָּה would respond to the claim that the Mishnah's oath is a rabbinic enactment. The question is whether the Sages would institute an oath that has no corresponding basis in Torah law.

keyTerms.meaning: And the other opinion, referring to רַב חִיָּה.
According to Torah law.

steps.whatToRemember: The question challenges the resolution by questioning the basis for a rabbinic oath.
steps.confusionAlert: The question is not a direct objection but a request for clarification on how רַב חִיָּה would view the resolution.
steps.whyThisMatters: This question tests the consistency of רַב חִיָּה's view with the resolution.

phrases.aramaic: וְאִידַּךְ? אִין תַּקָּנַת חֲכָמִים הִיא. וּמִיהוּ,
phrases.english: And the other? If it is a rabbinic ordinance. But,
If you say that by Torah law "here you are" is obligated –
The Sages instituted an oath like that of Torah law,
But if you say that by Torah law "here you are" is exempt –

phrases.aramaic: מְתַקְּנִי רַבָּנַן שְׁבוּעָה דְּלֵיתָא דִּכְווֹתַהּ בִּדְאוֹרָיְיתָא?
phrases.english: The Sages instituted an oath that has no counterpart in Torah law?

steps.triggerLanguage: וְאִידַךְ? אִין תַּקָּנַת חֲכָמִים הִיא. וּמִיהוּ, אִי אָמְרַתְּ בִּשְׁלָמָא מִדְּאוֹרַיְתָא ״הֵילָךְ״ חַיָּיב – מְתַקְנִי רַבָּנַן שְׁבוּעָה כְּעֵין דְּאוֹרַיְתָא, אֶלָּא אִי אָמְרַתְּ מִדְּאוֹרַיְתָא ״הֵילָךְ״ פָּטוּר – מְתַקְנִי רַבָּנַן שְׁבוּעָה דְלֵיתָא דִכְווֹתָהּ בִּדְאוֹרַיְתָא?

rashi.hebrew: אי אמרת בשלמא מדאורייתא חייב - כלומר גבי שבועה דאורייתא כגון בהודאה במקצת:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the phrase מדאורייתא חייב refers to a Torah-level obligation for an oath, such as in the case of a partial admission (הודאה במקצת).

steps.title: Introduction of a challenge from a baraita
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara introduces a challenge from a baraita to רבי חייא's view, setting up the next stage of the discussion.
steps.whatsHappening: The Gemara introduces a new challenge from a baraita. This is a setup line for the next stage of the discussion.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The Gemara introduces a challenge from a baraita to רַב חִיָּה's view. This sets up the next stage of the discussion, where the Gemara will explore whether רַב חִיָּה's view can withstand this new challenge.

keyTerms.meaning: The Gemara raises an objection or challenge.
A baraita, a teaching outside the Mishnah.

steps.whatToRemember: A new challenge from a baraita is introduced.
steps.confusionAlert: The challenge is not yet detailed, but it sets up the next stage of the discussion.
steps.whyThisMatters: This challenge tests the consistency of רַב חִיָּה's view with other sources.

phrases.english: He sat:
